heading: MODULUS OCTAVUS — Quot sint hospites illius civitatis. Concentus I
card_summary: This brief passage from the Eighth Mode opens with the question of how many "guests" inhabit the celestial city alongside the Trinity and its Queen. Zorzi cites Revelation (John's innumerable multitude), Genesis (God's promise to Abraham about the stars and his seed through Isaac), and Pseudo-Dionysius on the incomprehensibility of angelic number. He then critically surveys two scholastic attempts to calculate the total number of angels: one derived from squaring the legion-size of 6,666 across all nine choirs (yielding 399,920,004), and one drawn from Daniel's "thousands of thousands." Zorzi dismisses both methods as grounded in "mere imagination" or arithmetic incompetence, asserting that no reliable count of angels or the blessed can be obtained from these sources.
essay:
## Context and Opening Question

The opening *concentus* of the Eighth Mode poses the question *quot sint hospites illius civitatis* — how many guests inhabit that celestial city. The city in question is the heavenly Jerusalem already elaborated in earlier Modes, whose sovereign is the triune God (*trinum et unicum Principem summum*) together with the Queen (the Virgin Mary, or in Kabbalistic register, the *Shekhinah*). The question is whether the number of its angelic and blessed inhabitants can be determined.

## Scriptural Testimony on Innumerability

Zorzi opens with John's vision in the Apocalypse, citing the canonical Vulgate formulation:

> Vidi turbam magnam, quam dinumerare nemo poterat.

<cite>p. 2578</cite>

This is Revelation 7:9, the vision of the great multitude beyond counting. The citation establishes at the outset that Scripture itself counsels against any confident enumeration — the multitude is, by divine design, innumerable.

He then moves to Genesis and God's promise to Abraham — "count the stars, if you can; so shall your seed be" — clarifying through Paul (Galatians 4) that this seed is to be understood not carnally (not the offspring of the slave Hagar) but spiritually, through Isaac and the free woman, "by promise." The Pauline aside that "these things are said by allegory" (*quae sunt per allegoriam dicta*) is significant: it signals that the celestial arithmetic Zorzi is about to discuss must itself be read at an allegorical, not literal, level.

## Dionysius the Areopagite on Transcendent Number

Zorzi invokes Pseudo-Dionysius to place the angelic count definitively beyond human computation:

> hic tamen numerus (ut ait Dionysius) facultatem humanae supputationis transcendens cognitus est illi soli, qui capillos capitis omnes dinumerat.

<cite>p. 2578</cite>

The reference is to *The Celestial Hierarchy* or *The Divine Names*, where Dionysius stresses that angelic multitudes exceed any human calculus. Only God — the one who numbers even the hairs of the head (Matthew 10:30) — knows the true count. This Dionysian appeal serves Zorzi's larger apophatic strategy: what matters is not the number but the harmony and praise (*laudes principis*) that fill the celestial court.

## The Scholastic Calculation from Legion-Size

Zorzi then rehearses, only to dismiss, a scholastic attempt at angelic enumeration. The method proceeds as follows: a *legio* contains 6,666 (six thousand six hundred and sixty-six); each of the nine choirs contains as many legions as there are units in a legion when the legion-number is squared; therefore each choir contains 6,666² = 44,435,556; and across all nine choirs the total is 399,920,004.

> Dicunt enim in quolibet choro, qui novem sunt, tot esse legiones, quot unitates legio ipsa complectitur quadrando numerum legionis. Legio autem continet (ut aiunt) sex millia sexcentos et sexaginta sex.

<cite>p. 2578–2579</cite>

The choice of 6,666 as the legion-size carries obvious numerological resonance with 666, the number of the Beast in Revelation. Zorzi notes the arithmetic without pursuing that symbolic thread. He dismisses the entire scheme with characteristic brevity: *haec relinquemus tamquam fundata in mera imaginatione* — "we leave these things as founded in mere imagination."

## The Danielic Calculation and Its Arithmetical Error

A second tradition derives from Daniel 7:10: *millia millium ministrabant ei, et decies centena millia assistebant ei* — "thousands of thousands served him, and ten hundred thousands stood before him." Some interpreters took this as a literal census, yielding two thousand thousand (i.e., two million) from the first phrase. Zorzi then delivers a sharp polemical aside: a certain "most recent theologian among ours" had the audacity to assert that *decies centena millia* ("ten times a hundred thousand" = one million) is greater than *millia millium* ("thousands of thousands" = one million). Zorzi dismisses this blunder as proceeding from *omnimoda imperitia numerorum et supputationum* — "complete ignorance of numbers and calculations."

> quod decies centena millia plura sunt quam millia millium, quod ex omnimoda imperitia numerorum et supputationum procedit.

<cite>p. 2579</cite>

The target of this polemic is unnamed but the dismissal is unambiguous: both *millia millium* and *decies centena millia* equal one million, and the Danielic passage was never intended as an arithmetic census.

## Methodological Conclusion

The passage closes with a methodological verdict: neither the scholastic legion-calculation nor the Danielic tradition yields a reliable count of angels or the blessed. The first presupposes what it needed to prove; the second is misread by those who lack basic numeracy. True knowledge of angelic multitude belongs to God alone.

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

The passage is brief but strategically placed at the opening of the Eighth Mode's first *concentus*. It clears the ground for Zorzi's own approach to heavenly number — which will proceed not through arithmetic but through harmonic ratio and Kabbalistic correspondence. The Dionysian authority establishes that celestial number is suprational; the Pauline allegorical reading signals that what follows must be read symbolically. The dismissal of scholastic bean-counting sets Zorzi's Neoplatonic-Kabbalistic harmonics against the arid computational theology of the schools. The number 6,666 embedded in the legion calculation may also carry implicit resonance with Zorzi's ongoing meditation on the relationship between demonic and divine number — a thread running through the *De Harmonia Mundi* as a whole.
